name: investment
system_prompt: You are a senior investment analyst at a fund management firm. Analyze investment opportunities, assess risks, and make data-driven recommendations using the available tools. Always ground your analysis in concrete data before making recommendations.

tools.name: market_data_lookup
tools.description: Get current and historical price, volume, P/E ratio, market cap, and other market data for a stock or index

tools.name: financial_analysis
tools.description: Run fundamental analysis including earnings, revenue, margins, cash flow, and balance sheet metrics

tools.name: risk_assessment
tools.description: Calculate risk metrics including VaR, beta, Sharpe ratio, max drawdown, and correlation analysis

tools.name: news_search
tools.description: Search recent financial news, earnings calls, analyst reports, and SEC filings

tools.name: portfolio_optimizer
tools.description: Optimize portfolio allocation using modern portfolio theory, including efficient frontier and rebalancing

tools.name: sector_comparison
tools.description: Compare performance and fundamentals across sectors, industries, and peer groups

contrast_types.growth_vs_value: One request focuses on growth metrics (revenue growth, market expansion, TAM), the other on value metrics (P/E ratio, dividend yield, book value). Example: "Which tech stocks have the fastest revenue growth?" vs "Which tech stocks are trading below book value?"
contrast_types.fundamental_vs_technical: One request needs fundamental data (earnings, margins, cash flow), the other needs price/volume patterns (moving averages, RSI, support/resistance). Example: "What are AAPL's profit margins?" vs "Is AAPL above its 200-day moving average?"
contrast_types.single_stock_vs_portfolio: One request analyzes a single stock in isolation, the other optimizes across multiple positions. Example: "Analyze NVDA's growth prospects" vs "Rebalance my portfolio of NVDA, AAPL, and MSFT"
contrast_types.low_risk_vs_high_risk: One request seeks conservative/defensive investments, the other targets aggressive/high-growth opportunities. Example: "Find stable dividend stocks for retirement" vs "Find high-beta stocks with momentum"
contrast_types.short_term_vs_long_term: One request focuses on short-term trading (days to weeks), the other on long-term investing (years). Example: "What's the earnings play for next week?" vs "Which stocks are good 10-year holds?"
contrast_types.domestic_vs_international: One request targets domestic/US markets, the other targets international/emerging markets. Example: "Best US large-cap stocks" vs "Best emerging market opportunities"
